articles.title: IRS Enforcement Snapshot: High-Risk Schedules on 2025 Returns
articles.summary: A practitioner-focused overview of current IRS enforcement campaigns targeting high-risk schedules filed for 2025 individual and business returns.
articles.body: The IRS has announced new enforcement campaigns focusing on high-risk schedules, including Schedule C losses, large Schedule A charitable deductions, and international information reporting forms. Practitioners should review their internal review procedures, ensure contemporaneous documentation is maintained, and proactively communicate with clients who have historically triggered correspondence exams...

articles.title: Field Examination Reboot: What Small Firms Need to Know in 2026
articles.summary: IRS field exam procedures are evolving with more data analytics and centralized case selection. Learn how to prepare your small firm.
articles.body: Beginning in 2026, the IRS is rolling out updated field examination procedures that rely more heavily on pre-contact analytics and centralized case building. This article summarizes the new IRM guidance, outlines likely triggers for field exams, and provides a preparation checklist you can adapt for your firm...

articles.title: IRS Collections: Fresh Start Thresholds and Installment Agreements in 2026
articles.summary: Updated IRS collection thresholds affect when your clients qualify for streamlined installment agreements and other Fresh Start relief.
articles.body: The IRS has quietly updated several internal thresholds used to determine eligibility for streamlined installment agreements and other Fresh Start initiative programs. While the formal dollar limits have not changed in the Code, internal tolerances and documentation requirements have shifted. This enforcement update breaks down the new thresholds and provides sample client scenarios...

articles.title: LB&I Campaigns Targeting Cross-Border Partnerships
articles.summary: A deeper look at LB&I enforcement campaigns involving complex cross-border partnership structures.
articles.body: The IRS Large Business & International division continues to refine its campaign approach, with several active campaigns focused on cross-border partnerships and hybrid entities. Practitioners should be aware of the key issues examiners are trained to spot, including allocation methodologies, withholding compliance, and reporting on Schedules K-2 and K-3...

articles.title: Building a Deadline Management System for a 3-Person Tax Firm
articles.summary: Practical strategies and tools for tracking client deadlines in a very small tax practice.
articles.body: When your entire compliance practice rests on a team of three, every missed deadline matters. This article walks through three models for deadline tracking: practice management software, spreadsheet-based systems, and shared calendar workflows. We include sample task lists, escalation rules, and client communication templates...

articles.title: Top 10 Ethics Pitfalls for Tax Practitioners in 2026
articles.summary: From client due diligence to managing conflicts of interest, we outline the ethics traps most likely to trigger discipline.
articles.body: Ethics issues rarely arise from a single bad decision. More often, they grow from incremental shortcuts that slowly become normal. In this article, we review ten common ethics pitfalls, including engagement letter gaps, inadequate documentation for positions taken, and overly aggressive reliance on client representations...

articles.title: Choosing the Right Entity Type for Growing Business Clients
articles.summary: A framework for helping clients evaluate S corporations, partnerships, and C corporations under current law.
articles.body: Entity selection is no longer a one-time decision. With qualified business income rules, state tax workarounds, and evolving exit strategies, practitioners must revisit entity choice regularly. This article provides comparison charts, cash-flow modeling examples, and talking points for client meetings...

articles.title: State Tax Nexus Rules: A 2025–2026 Practitioner Guide
articles.summary: An overview of economic nexus thresholds and registration triggers across key states.
articles.body: Economic nexus standards have proliferated across income, franchise, and sales taxes. This guide summarizes threshold levels in major states, highlights common traps for remote sellers and professional services firms, and includes a client questionnaire you can adapt for nexus reviews...

articles.title: Video: Workflow Automation Tools for Solo & Small Firms
articles.summary: A 30-minute walkthrough of affordable workflow automation tools tailored to small tax practices.

articles.title: Podcast: Marketing Your Tax Practice in the Off-Season
articles.summary: In this 45-minute episode, we discuss practical marketing strategies you can implement after filing season.

articles.title: Correspondence Exams: Trends Emerging from 2024 Filing Season
articles.summary: Preliminary observations on correspondence examination trends from the prior filing season.
articles.body: Early data from 2024 returns indicates the IRS is continuing to prioritize automated underreporter notices and math-error adjustments. This article summarizes the most common triggers and how to respond efficiently...

articles.title: Comparing Top Practice Management Platforms for 2026
articles.summary: A feature-by-feature comparison of leading practice management tools for small tax firms.
articles.body: Selecting practice management software is one of the highest-impact technology decisions a firm can make. We compare leading tools on deadlines, workflow automation, client portals, and integrations with tax and accounting software...

articles.title: Planning Your CPE Path: Ethics, Tax, and Advisory Skills
articles.summary: How to build a balanced CPE plan that meets requirements and supports your long-term career goals.
articles.body: Too many practitioners treat CPE as a compliance exercise. This article lays out a three-part planning framework: technical tax, ethics and risk management, and advisory/communication skills...

articles.title: IRS Enforcement Roundup: Crypto, Gig Economy, and High-Income Non-Filers
articles.summary: A quarterly roundup of IRS enforcement actions and campaign activity in emerging areas.
articles.body: The IRS continues to invest in enforcement around digital assets, platform-based gig work, and high-income non-filers. This roundup summarizes recent announcements, court cases, and data-sharing initiatives that will impact your clients...

articles.title: Getting the Most from Your Member Directory Profile
articles.summary: Tips for writing a compelling profile, highlighting specialties, and selecting licensed states.
articles.body: Your member directory profile is often a prospect's first impression of your practice. We walk through how to describe your specialties, choose which states to show, and craft a concise professional title...

forum_threads.subject: What tools are you using to track 4/15 and 10/15 deadlines?
forum_threads.body: We are a two-preparer firm and still using a color-coded spreadsheet to track individual and business return deadlines. It works, but it feels fragile. Curious what others are using for tracking extensions vs. filed returns (especially for 4/15 and 10/15). Do you rely on practice management software, reminders in your tax prep system, or something else?

forum_threads.subject: Best way to delegate e-file acknowledgments in a 3-person firm?
forum_threads.body: We just added a third team member this year and I'm struggling with how to delegate the daily e-file ACK review. Right now I (the owner) still log in and clear everything. How are other small firms delegating this without losing oversight?

forum_threads.subject: Flat-fee vs hourly for IRS representation work
forum_threads.body: For small-dollar IRS representation (CP2000s, basic installment agreements, etc.), do you prefer flat-fee pricing or hourly? Ive been doing hourly but clients keep asking for a fixed number. Would love to hear what structures are working for other small firms.

forum_threads.subject: Handling crypto 1099 mismatches on 2025 returns
forum_threads.body: Seeing a lot more crypto 1099 reporting this year, and the basis info is often incomplete. How are you documenting positions when the 1099-B or 1099-DA is missing key details, and how worried are you about IRS matching notices?

forum_threads.subject: Favorite organizers for 1040 clients who hate paper
forum_threads.body: We have a lot of tech-friendly clients who truly hate paper organizers. If youre using a digital organizer or questionnaire, what are you using and how well is it working during busy season?

forum_threads.subject: Reasonable comp benchmarks for S corp owners in small professional firms
forum_threads.body: Looking for resources or rules of thumb on reasonable salary vs distributions for small professional S corps (11 owners, revenue under $750k). Do you document using surveys, or just narrative memos?

forum_threads.subject: Which entity type are you recommending for new 1-owner consulting businesses?
forum_threads.body: When a solo consultant comes in with no employees and projected revenue around $150k, what entity types are you actually recommending in 2025/2026? Are you still steering many towards S corps or leaving them as Schedule C for a while?

forum_threads.subject: Anyone seeing more inquiries on Schedule C losses under $30k?
forum_threads.body: During the last filing season I saw a spike in correspondence exams focusing on Schedule C losses, even when they were well under $30k. Curious whether others are seeing similar patterns and whether they seem tied to particular industries or codes.

forum_threads.subject: Best practices for documenting client hardship in collection cases
forum_threads.body: Looking for sample checklists or templates you use to document hardship when requesting currently-not-collectible status or more favorable payment terms. Do you have clients sign anything acknowledging how their info will be used?

forum_threads.subject: Comparing Workflow360 vs generic project tools (Asana/Trello)
forum_threads.body: For those of you running 35 person firms, have you found value in tax-specific workflow tools like Workflow360 compared to generic tools like Asana or Trello? Curious about deadline features, e-sign integrations, and how painful the initial setup was.

forum_threads.subject: Client portals that small firms actually like
forum_threads.body: Most of the built-in portals from tax prep vendors feel clunky. Has anyone found a client portal solution that clients actually use and that doesn't create a ton of support tickets for password resets?

forum_threads.subject: How often do you raise rates for legacy 1040 clients?
forum_threads.body: We have 60+ 1040 clients who have been with us 810 years and are still paying 2018 pricing. For those of you in similar situations, do you gradually increase each year, or do a larger reset every few years? Any sample client communications would be appreciated.

forum_posts.body: OP here 3 we currently have one master spreadsheet with columns for client name, form type, entity type, extension filed (Y/N + date), docs received, prep started, review complete, e-file date, and payment status. It9s stored in OneDrive with color-coding for status. It technically works, but we9ve already had one scare with someone sorting only a single column. I9m wondering if it9s time to move to a true practice management system that can surface just 4/15 and 10/15 deadlines and send reminders.

We moved from a shared spreadsheet to Workflow360 last year (using the member discount) and it was a big improvement. We created separate workflows for 91040 3 Original9 and 9Business Returns9 with due dates anchored to 4/15, 3/15, and 9/15/10/15. The system auto-calculates extension deadlines, and we use tags for 9extension filed9 vs 9original filed9. The key was spending a weekend building templates for each return type.

If you want to stay in Excel for a bit longer, lock down your header row and use data validation lists for status instead of colors. We also turned the sheet into a proper Excel table and trained everyone to filter instead of sorting. It9s still not as good as a dedicated system, but it eliminated the 9sorted one column9 disaster you mentioned.

Right now I9m the only one clearing e-file acknowledgments each morning. We use the tax software dashboard plus an internal Slack channel where I post any rejects and assign them. As volume grows, I9m not sure if I should fully hand off ACK review to staff or keep a daily oversight role (e.g., I only review rejects and late ACKs). Curious how other small firms structure this.

For a basic CP2000 response or a simple installment agreement request, I currently bill hourly at a reduced rate and take a retainer. Clients keep asking for 9all-in9 pricing. I9m considering a three-tier flat-fee menu (notices under $10k, $10k30k, and >$30k) but I9m worried about scope creep. How are you defining the engagement so flat fees don9t turn into unlimited email support?

I use flat fees but the engagement letter is very explicit. My base fee includes: reviewing the notice, requesting and reviewing the transcript, one round of written response, and one 30-minute call. Anything beyond that (appeals, multiple follow-up letters) is billed hourly. I spell this out in an IRS representation engagement letter and have clients initial the section on scope.

I9m seeing more 1099-DA and platform summaries that don9t match what clients actually did on the exchanges. Sometimes the client pulled data into a third-party tax report that looks more accurate than the broker form. I9m documenting assumptions in my workpapers, but still worried about mismatch notices. Are you attaching detailed CSVs, or just keeping everything in your file and responding if/when a notice comes?

I do not attach the CSVs unless the situation is unusual. I document the reconciliation in my workpapers, keep a copy of the exchange reports, and make sure the return is internally consistent (e.g., totals tie to what the IRS will see numerically). If a CP2000 shows up, I use the documentation to prepare a response. The key for me is a short memo explaining why the 1099 is incomplete and what source we considered more reliable.

Most of our younger clients either ignore paper organizers or send us random screenshots instead. We9ve experimented with a fillable PDF, but even that doesn9t get used consistently. Thinking about switching to a shorter online questionnaire that just asks the 9what changed from last year?9 questions and then using last year9s return as the main checklist.

I mostly serve small professional S corps (consultants, EAs, CPAs). Right now I document reasonable comp using a narrative memo plus a couple of salary survey screenshots (RCReports, BLS). I9m curious whether others are still using more formal third-party reports or if a well-documented internal memo has been sufficient in practice.

I have a steady stream of new one-owner consulting businesses with expected revenue between $120k and $200k. In prior years I almost always recommended a single-member LLC taxed as an S corp once they stabilized. Lately I9m hesitating because of compliance overhead and state issues (CA, NY, etc.). Wondering what others are doing given current QBI rules and state pass-through entity taxes.

For my consulting clients I usually keep them as Schedule C for year one unless they9re clearly going to exceed ~$150k and have low overhead. Once revenue is more predictable, we model S corp vs Schedule C, including state payroll and PTET implications. I also make sure the engagement letter covers an annual 9entity review9 so we revisit the choice instead of treating it as a one-time decision.

In the last filing season I received several correspondence exams on Schedule C losses that were in the $10k30k range with clear business purpose and good documentation. A few were hobby-loss themed, but others just requested basic substantiation. I9m trying to determine whether this is a new campaign or just random selection. Anyone else seeing this, and are you changing how you document small Schedule C losses?

Yes, definitely seeing more of these. I9ve started adding a brief 9business activity9 summary to my workpapers for any recurring Schedule C loss (even under $20k) 3 what the business actually does, how the client markets it, and what changed from prior years. I also make sure engagement letters and organizers ask specific questions about time spent and profit motive so I have something contemporaneous if a notice arrives.

When requesting CNC or more flexible installment terms, I9m gathering the usual Form 433 info plus bank statements and pay stubs. I9m curious how detailed your internal hardship documentation is and whether you use a standard checklist. Also, do you have clients sign any acknowledgment about how sensitive financial information will be used and stored?

We9re demoing Workflow360 this month. On paper it looks great (templates, deadline dashboards, integrations with our tax software), but I9m worried about the learning curve for my two staff members who are used to living in spreadsheets and email. If you made the switch from Trello/Asana, how long did it take before the team stopped fighting the new system?

We moved from Trello to Workflow360 last summer. It took about a month to get comfortable and a full busy season before we really trusted the dashboards. What helped: (1) we mapped our existing Trello boards to Workflow360 workflows before go-live, (2) I appointed one 9workflow champion9 on the team, and (3) we ran both systems in parallel for two weeks so staff could see the benefits without feeling like the rug was pulled out from under them.

We9ve tried three different client portals from tax software vendors and the feedback has been mixed: clients like secure upload but hate managing yet another login. Considering a standalone portal that supports SSO or magic links. Has anyone found a solution that actually reduced email volume instead of adding to it?

We have a core group of 1040 clients who have been with us since we opened the firm and are still paying what they paid in 2018. I know we need to raise rates, but I9m nervous about shocking them. Do you raise a little bit every year, or do a bigger reset every few years? Any sample language for explaining the change would be helpful.

I do small annual increases for most clients (35%) and a bigger reset every 45 years when scope has clearly expanded. My email usually says something like: 9We9ve reviewed our pricing to reflect increased complexity, technology costs, and year-round support. For the upcoming filing season, your base fee will be $X. This keeps us in line with market rates while continuing to provide the level of service you9re used to.9 I also give long-term clients a slightly smaller increase than new ones.
